narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 sits a bit below the usual 3.21, and your lower-middle family income bracket reflects real-world challenges many face. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in this dataset. The university's model predicted a 75% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection of your law school application to prioritize spots for those seen as lower-risk for bar passage. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance lagged behind most peers who pass the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, again under the typical 5.71, reinforcing concerns about consistent early achievement compared to successful bar takers. Your LSAT score worked in your favor as the third-most influential factor, pushing the odds toward passing since it's stronger than average and a key strength for many who succeed. Overall, these academic deciles outweighed your LSAT edge, placing you outside the profile of typical students who pass—those often in higher deciles with balanced records. While your background as a white male doesn't stand out as unusual here, the model zeroed in on these performance gaps as the core risks. This isn't a judgment on your potential, just a data-driven call based on patterns from past students. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement in upper-year courses or additional credentials like postgraduate work. Consider retaking the LSAT if possible to widen that positive margin, or seek programs valuing holistic reviews over strict predictions. Many in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these exact areas—your path forward is still very much open.
